sourceText: Birth of Flavius Petrus Sabbatius in Tauresium.
Ascends to the throne as Emperor Justinian I.
The Nika riots erupt in Constantinople and are brutally suppressed.
The Corpus Juris Civilis (Body of Civil Law) is compiled and published.
General Belisarius reconquers North Africa from the Vandals.
The rebuilt Hagia Sophia is consecrated in Constantinople.
The Plague of Justinian begins, a pandemic that devastates the empire's population.
The Gothic War concludes, with Byzantium nominally controlling Italy after a long and destructive conflict.
Death of Justinian I in Constantinople.
Corpus Juris Civilis
The monumental codification of all Roman law, which became the foundation for modern civil law systems in much of the world.
Renovatio imperii Romanorum
The ambitious program of 'restoration of the Empire', which led to the partial reconquest of the Western Roman Empire.
Architectural Program
Commissioned hundreds of churches and secular buildings across the empire, most famously the Hagia Sophia in Constantinople.
Was Justinian truly a 'Roman' emperor?

Yes. He was the ruler of the Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantium), which was the direct continuation of the Roman Empire. His native language was Latin, and he saw himself as the rightful heir to the Caesars, dedicated to restoring the empire's lost territories.
What was the Nika Riot?

A massive urban uprising in 532 AD, sparked by chariot racing factions (the Blues and Greens), that grew into a revolt against Justinian's high taxes and policies. It nearly forced him to flee but was brutally suppressed, resulting in tens of thousands of deaths.
How influential was his wife, Theodora?

Immensely. Rising from a humble background, she became his most trusted advisor and co-ruler. Her refusal to flee during the Nika riots is credited with saving his reign, and she heavily influenced religious and social policies, particularly those affecting women.
Born in Moscow to Grand Prince Vasily II.
Ascends to the throne as Grand Prince of Moscow.
Defeats the army of the Novgorod Republic at the Battle of Shelon River.
Formally annexes the Republic of Novgorod, ending its independence.
The Great Stand on the Ugra River, which marks the de facto end of the Mongol-Tatar yoke over Russia.
Annexes the Principality of Tver, his last major Russian rival.
Issues the Sudebnik, a new code of laws for the unified state.
Dies in Moscow, leaving a vastly expanded and centralized state to his son, Vasily III.
Gathering of the Russian Lands
He more than tripled the territory of Moscow by annexing rival principalities like Novgorod and Tver.
End of the Mongol Yoke
He successfully repudiated the authority of the Golden Horde after the Great Stand on the Ugra River in 1480.
Centralization of Power
He created the Sudebnik of 1497, a new legal code that standardized law and strengthened the authority of the Grand Prince.
Architectural Legacy
He oversaw the major reconstruction of the Moscow Kremlin, inviting Italian architects to build new cathedrals and palaces.
Why is he called 'the Great'?

He is called 'the Great' for his pivotal role in ending over 200 years of Mongol dominance and for unifying the disparate Russian principalities into a single, powerful state, laying the foundation for modern Russia.
What was the Great Stand on the Ugra River?

It was a military standoff in 1480 between Ivan III's forces and the Golden Horde. Ivan's refusal to back down or pay tribute, leading to the Horde's withdrawal, marked the de facto independence of Muscovy.
How did his marriage to Sophia Palaiologina affect Russia?

His marriage to the niece of the last Byzantine emperor, Sophia Palaiologina, brought Byzantine court rituals, the title of 'Tsar' (Caesar), and the double-headed eagle emblem to Moscow, reinforcing Moscow's claim as the spiritual and political successor to the Byzantine Empire ('Third Rome').
What is the Sudebnik of 1497?
It was a code of laws Ivan III introduced to create a uniform legal system across his newly unified territories. It standardized judicial procedures and strengthened the central government's control.
Becomes Grand Prince of Moscow at age three.
Crowned as the first 'Tsar of All Russia'.
Conquers the Khanate of Kazan.
Conquers the Khanate of Astrakhan.
His wife, Anastasia Romanovna, dies, marking a turn in his personality.
Establishes the Oprichnina, a period of mass repression.
Leads the Massacre of Novgorod.
Kills his eldest son and heir, Ivan Ivanovich, in a fit of rage.
Dies, leaving the throne to his younger, unfit son Feodor.
First Tsar of All Russia
He was the first Muscovite ruler to officially adopt the title of Tsar, elevating Russia's status on the world stage.
Conquest of Kazan and Astrakhan
His military campaigns successfully annexed the Tatar khanates, securing the entire Volga River for Russia and opening the way for eastward expansion.
Centralization of the Russian State
Through legal reforms (Sudebnik of 1550) and the violent suppression of the boyar aristocracy, he consolidated power and laid the foundations for Russian autocracy.
Why was he called 'the Terrible'?

The Russian epithet, 'Grozny' (Грозный), more accurately translates to 'fearsome,' 'formidable,' or 'awe-inspiring.' It referred to his power and authority, though his later acts of cruelty and paranoia made the English translation 'the Terrible' stick.
What was the Oprichnina?

The Oprichnina was a state policy and territory (1565-1572) where Ivan IV ruled directly, using a personal force of bodyguards (the Oprichniki) to purge and execute those he deemed traitors, primarily the boyar aristocracy.
Did he really kill his son?

Most historical accounts, supported by contemporary sources and later evidence, confirm that Ivan struck his son, Ivan Ivanovich, with a pointed staff during a heated argument, inflicting a fatal wound.
Born in Mazepyntsi, near Bila Tserkva, then part of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Elected Hetman of the Zaporizhian Host of Left-bank Ukraine.
Ruled as Hetman, initially as a loyal vassal to Tsar Peter the Great of Russia, and became a great patron of arts and culture.
Briefly united both Left-bank and Right-bank Ukraine under his rule during an uprising against Poland.
Secretly allied with Charles XII of Sweden against Peter the Great during the Great Northern War.
Suffered a decisive defeat alongside the Swedes at the Battle of Poltava.
Died in exile in Bendery (Tighina), then part of the Ottoman Empire.
Cultural Patronage
He funded the construction of numerous churches and educational institutions in the 'Mazepa Baroque' style, fostering a cultural renaissance.
Unification Effort
In 1704, he briefly united most of ethnic Ukrainian lands (Left-bank and Right-bank Ukraine) under his rule for the first time in decades.
Symbol of Independence
His dramatic break with Russia made him a powerful and enduring symbol of the Ukrainian struggle for national independence.
Why is Mazepa considered a traitor in Russia but a hero in Ukraine?

In Russia, he is seen as a traitor for breaking his oath of allegiance to Tsar Peter the Great. In Ukraine, he is widely celebrated as a national hero who made a courageous attempt to secure independence from Russian domination.
What was the 'Mazepa Baroque'?

It is a specific style of Ukrainian Baroque architecture that flourished in the late 17th and early 18th centuries. It is named after Mazepa due to his extensive patronage of church construction and the arts during his rule as Hetman.
What happened after the Battle of Poltava?

After the decisive defeat, Mazepa and Charles XII fled to the Ottoman Empire to seek refuge. Mazepa died shortly after in the city of Bendery. Peter the Great severely curtailed the autonomy of the Hetmanate, tightening Russian control over Ukraine.
Born in Nahuievychi, Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria (modern-day Ukraine).
Enrolls at Lviv University and begins publishing his first literary works.
First arrested for socialist activities and belonging to a secret society.
Co-founds the Ruthenian-Ukrainian Radical Party, the first modern Ukrainian political party.
Defends his doctoral dissertation, 'Barlaam and Josaphat', at the University of Vienna.
Publishes his philosophical epic poem 'Moses' (Мойсей), a cornerstone of his literary legacy.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Dies in Lviv at the age of 59, impoverished but revered as a national icon.
Prolific Literary Output
He wrote over 6,000 works, including poetry, prose, drama, and translations, significantly shaping modern Ukrainian literature.
Political Activism
He co-founded the first modern Ukrainian political party and consistently advocated for socialism and Ukrainian national rights.
Scholarly Contributions
He authored numerous academic works in history, ethnography, and literary criticism, earning a doctorate from the University of Vienna.
Master Translator
He translated approximately 200 authors from 14 languages into Ukrainian, including works by Shakespeare, Goethe, and Dante.
Why is he called 'Kameniar'?

It means 'The Stone-Breaker' in Ukrainian, from his famous poem of the same name. It symbolizes his life's work of breaking the metaphorical stones of oppression to forge a path to freedom for his people.
Was he ever a professor?

Despite earning his doctorate and being eminently qualified, the Austro-Hungarian authorities blocked his appointment to a professorship at Lviv University due to his socialist and pro-Ukrainian political activism.
Did he win the Nobel Prize?

He was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in late 1915, and his candidacy was highly regarded. However, he died in May 1916 before the committee could make its decision, and the prize is not awarded posthumously.
What was his relationship with the Russian Empire?
He was a sharp critic of Russian tsarist autocracy and its oppressive policies towards the Ukrainian language and culture. His works were often banned in the Russian Empire.
